channel.title: Master Feed (Updated every 30 mins)
channel.description: Master Feed (Updated every 30 mins) generated by script

item.title: ‘Project Hail Mary’ and the Politics of Science Fiction
item.description: Even the most entertaining tale carries a political message.

item.title: Saffron’s Last Gasp
item.description: The Iran war is choking supply of the world’s most expensive spice.

item.title: The Real Origin of the World’s Most Famous Female Serial Killer
item.description: A new book explores the geopolitical scheming that created the blood countess legend.

item.title: ‘Hokum’ Is Haunted by Ireland’s Dark History
item.description: A new horror film reckons with the country’s buried sins—and the women erased by them.

item.title: What in the World?
item.description: Test yourself on the week of April 25: Iran offers to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, the UAE leaves OPEC, and Colombia hosts a conference.

item.title: Two Novels Take on the Post-American Dream
item.description: Plus, more international fiction releases in May.

item.title: Does acupuncture work?
item.description: It seems useful for pain. The jury’s out on everything else

item.title: Labour faces a drubbing in England’s local elections
item.description: The party could lose more than half of the council seats it is defending

item.title: 5 Years Since UK Parliament Recognized the Uyghur Genocide, There’s Been Little Policy Action
item.description: Britain recognized a genocide, then did nothing to stop it.

item.title: Strategic Autonomy or a Pro-US Tilt? India’s Foreign Policy Under Pressure
item.description: The Iran war is forcing India to confront the tension between strategic autonomy and its commitment to the Indo-Pacific framework.

item.title: Israel’s Slow War on the West Bank
item.description: Israeli leaders seem increasingly comfortable with the collapse of the Palestinian Authority.

item.title: China Never Actually Removed Homosexuality From Its Official List of Mental Disorders
item.description: On the 25th anniversary of the Chinese Classification of Mental Disorders Version 3 (CCMD-3), we revisit the widely repeated claim that China “removed” homosexuality from its list of mental disorders in 2001 – and make the case for retiring it.

item.title: The BRICS Climate Stress Test: India’s Opportunity to Reshape Global Climate Finance Architecture
item.description: BRICS is a climate-critical grouping whether it chooses to be or not.

item.title: Has De-Dollarization Begun?
item.description: US President Donald Trump’s military adventures, attacks on long-standing allies, and dismantling of institutions like USAID are eroding the trust on which the dollar’s global primacy ultimately rests. The world’s reserve currency may have already begun its long, slow decline.

item.title: Why China’s Internet Is Reopening the Case Against the ‘Qin System’ of the First Emperor
item.description: In niche corners of the Chinese internet, discussions of the very first dynasty stand in for critiques of today’s China.

item.title: A US-India Partnership for the AI Age
item.description: Although the United States maintains a decisive edge in frontier AI models and high-end compute, China has proven that its capacity for innovation is a force to be reckoned with. To prevent the Chinese government from claiming the strategic high ground, the US needs the talent and data that only India can provide.

item.title: The Deeper Pattern Behind China’s Military Purges
item.description: Xi’s new commanders are the men his last generals blocked.

item.title: After Hungary, Could Mexico Be Next?
item.description: During his 16-year rule, Hungary's Viktor Orbán could plausibly argue that his model delivered order, stability, and rising living standards. But Mexico's government can make no such claim, suggesting that the country might find itself facing a destabilizing political transition sooner than expected.

item.title: Bangladesh’s Air Power: Intentions Meet a Widening Capability Gap
item.description: The Bangladesh Air Force risks becoming a paper tiger, if it isn’t already.

item.title: Why Trump Might Come to Regret the Iran War
item.description: He hoped it would be transformational; instead, it’s just one more round in the conflict.

item.title: The war in Iran has sent American inflation sharply higher
item.description: Our predictive index has reached a sizzling 3.6%

item.title: The Price of Peace With Iran
item.description: Washington must be willing to make uncomfortable concessions.

item.title: The China-US Clash Over Critical Minerals Is an Opportunity for Kazakhstan
item.description: Amid China-U.S. competition over critical minerals, Kazakhstan is positioned to become a key partner for Washington.

item.title: Why China Treats ‘Lying Flat’ as a National Security Threat
item.description: It's not about the economy at all: In a system that defines not struggling as a crime, opting out is a public offense.

item.title: Are the Ryukyu Islands an Overlooked Flashpoint in the Indo-Pacific?
item.description: Considerable attention has been paid to the threat of a Chinese invasion of Taiwan, but analysts have arguably overlooked a more immediate danger facing the Ryukyu Island chain.

item.title: The Real Meaning of the UAE’s OPEC Exit
item.description: The geopolitical realignment goes much deeper than just oil markets.

item.title: The Real Role of a Trump-Xi Meeting
item.description: The summit is unlikely to deliver decisive breakthroughs. Instead, its importance lies in how it helps manage competition under pressure.

item.title: Kazakhstan’s Nuclear Bet to Reach Carbon Neutrality
item.description: Despite plans to go from zero to four nuclear power plants by 2050, Kazakhstan’s strategy comes with significant challenges.

item.title: Colombia’s Anti-Fossil Fuel Conference
item.description: The country is trying to push for a green transition despite gridlock at U.N. climate talks.

item.title: How Many Divisions Has Pope Leo?
item.description: Following the Trump administration's spat with Pope Leo XIV over its misbegotten war with Iran, one is reminded of previous historical episodes when the Vatican offered moral clarity for a world that had been corrupted by cynicism and aggressive authoritarianism. Americans, above all, should heed the message.

item.title: What China Will Learn from Orbán’s Defeat
item.description: After watching Nikita Khrushchev’s repudiation of Stalin lead to a revolution in Hungary, Mao Zedong concluded that under one-party rule, no amount of political liberalization is safe. The defeat of Viktor Orbán in Hungary’s parliamentary elections last month most likely reinforced that lesson for Chinese President Xi Jinping.

item.title: Trump’s Plan B for Tariffs Rests on Shaky Foundations
item.description: The Section 301 case outlined by U.S. trade officials is neither coherent nor defensible.

item.title: Malaysia’s Tech Talent Shortage
item.description: Industry leaders describe a war for talent.

item.title: The Universal Uprising
item.description: Rather than a story of passive suffering, the history of slavery in the Americas is one of rebellion, solidarity, and democratic invention. A recent book traces the depth and creativity of enslaved people’s resistance, whose legacy continues to inspire social-justice movements around the world.

item.title: The Philippines Joins Washington’s Pax Silica
item.description: It's clear what the United States is getting out of its new critical minerals initiative, but less obvious how Manila stands to gain from it.

item.title: Libya’s False Peace
item.description: Libya needs political unity, not Washington’s dealmaking.

item.title: China and America Are Courting Nuclear Catastrophe
item.description: The consequences of Beijing’s weapons buildup.

item.title: When Myanmar Exported Federalism
item.description: After World War II, Burma helped design a federal constitution for Ethiopia and Eritrea, while fighting a civil war with ethnic minorities demanding the same at home.

item.title: 3D-Printed Weapons: An Emerging Problem in Southeast Asia?
item.description: While traditional firearms are currently a more pressing problem, seizures of 3DPFs are on the rise, with worrying implications for the region.

item.title: Cambodian Opposition Leader Loses Appeal Against Treason Conviction
item.description: The Phnom Penh Appeals Court upheld Kem Sokha's 27-year house arrest sentence, while imposing a further five-year travel ban.

item.title: Myanmar’s Ousted Leader Aung San Suu Kyi Transferred to House Arrest: State Media
item.description: The announcement is part of a concerted PR push currently being undertaken by the new "civilian" government in Naypyidaw.

item.title: The View From Europe
item.description: Belgian Defense Minister Theo Francken on Hegseth, Hormuz, and NATO.

item.title: Iran War Nears 60-Day War Powers Deadline
item.description: But U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth argues that the cease-fire has paused the clock.

item.title: Manila’s Piecemeal Military Modernization
item.description: For over 30 years, the Philippines has tried – and struggled – to bolster its defense capabilities. What went wrong?

item.title: Giorgia Meloni’s Populist Formula Failed
item.description: The Italian prime minister hasn’t convincingly delivered the renewal she once promised.

item.title: High-Rises Amid the Rubble in Syria
item.description: Who is Sharaa’s investment campaign really for?

item.title: ‘Everything Depends on Our Attitudes’
item.description: Belgian Defense Minister Theo Francken on Hegseth, Hormuz, and NATO’s future.

item.title: Time to edit some biological metaphors
item.description: Not everything is in your DNA

item.title: The Real Private-Credit Risk Is Opacity, Not Leverage
item.description: Credit risk is migrating beyond the traditional banking system into a sprawling network of funds, insurers, and lenders, with no single authority able to see the whole picture. The challenge facing policymakers is to ensure that risks remain visible as they shift into corners of the financial system not designed to absorb them.

item.title: Former Kyrgyz Security Chief Charged With Coup-plotting and Abuse of Office
item.description: Tashiev has not yet been detained, but has reportedly been instructed not to leave town.

item.title: How the Dreams of Bangladesh’s Student Protestors Died Young
item.description: Almost two years after Bangladesh's July uprising, its foot soldiers are learning what it means when a revolution eats its own.

item.title: US Deep-Sea Mining Policy Is Eroding Its Pacific Partnerships
item.description: The current unilateral approach may secure minerals in the short term, but it will erode the Pacific partnerships and deterrence architecture the United States needs for long-term security.

item.title: A New Economics for the 21st Century
item.description: By coming out in favor of industrial policy after many decades of advising against government intervention in the economy, the World Bank has taken an important step. But it still has much further to go to translate real-world evidence into better policy advice.

item.title: Warning Signs: How China Normalizes Its Military Presence
item.description: As its presence becomes more normal, China’s aggressive and illegal behavior will attract less opposition.

item.title: Pakistan Moves Toward a Sea-Denial Strategy
item.description: With its latest missile tests, the Pakistan Navy is operationalizing an A2/AD naval strategy that prioritizes precision strike capabilities.

item.description: Choke points on the supply of AI are changing its economics

item.title: Is the EU the Next Migration Destination for Uzbeks?
item.description: EU countries are becoming increasingly attractive destinations for Uzbek migrants, driven by higher wages, stronger labor protections, and adherence to international labor standards.

item.title: An Ayatollah for the Aggrieved
item.description: For Iran’s Sunni admirers, resistance remains the appeal.

item.title: An Economic Model for Securing Hormuz
item.description: With a military solution to the closure of the Strait unlikely to succeed, the only sustainable long-term arrangement is to institutionalize today’s emerging arrangement, by which Iran, in coordination with the Gulf states, guarantees safe passage for a fee. Such a system could benefit all parties—not least the United States.

item.title: Australia’s Foreign Minister Makes Critical Visits to Northeast Asia
item.description: Stopping in Japan, South Korea, and China, Foreign Minister Penny Wong met with three of Australia’s four largest trading partners.

item.title: Who Lost America’s Universities?
item.description: Lost in all the debates about whether US higher education is serving its proper function are conservative critics’ own motives. Leveraging the public’s apparent loss of confidence in colleges and universities, the American right has shifted the discussion from academic freedom to free speech, using the latter to undermine the former.

item.description: A REDD+ project in the country's northeast aims to conserve forest and reduce carbon emissions, but many local residents say they know little about it and never gave their consent.

item.description: The decision could pave the way for the 76-year-old's return to politics, but the future of his Pheu Thai Party is uncertain following a poor return at this year's general election.

item.description: Washington has finally woken up to the scale of the challenge, but there is a lot more it can do to punish perpetrators and support victims.

item.description: This year is set to witness a flowering of relations between Bangkok and Beijing – but Thai strategists remain both ambivalent and wary.

item.description: Plaintiffs argued that Facebook helped spread hate speech that “amounted to a substantial cause, and eventual perpetuation of, the Rohingya genocide.”

item.description: The U.N. nuclear watchdog suspects that roughly half of Tehran’s stockpile is still at its Isfahan facility.

item.description: The technology could tackle diseases such as atherosclerosis and hepatitis B

item.description: Democrats could try picking a young candidate who has a solid record of running a big city

item.title: Beware of Government by AI
item.description: As countries like the United Arab Emirates increasingly delegate decision-making to algorithms, often with little transparency, the risks are no longer hypothetical. Evidence shows these systems can cause widespread harm, erode democratic accountability, and concentrate power in the hands of tech titans.

item.description: Binyamin Netanyahu still has one big advantage over his opponents

item.description: Insights from Tom Wells.

item.title: Between IMF Conditions and Rising Prices, Is Sri Lanka Heading Toward Stagflation?
item.description: If inflation is driven primarily by rising costs rather than demand, this could undermine Sri Lanka’s fragile economic recovery.

item.title: The United States Is Losing the Race for Central Asia’s Critical Minerals
item.description: Without a sustained and coordinated push, the United States risks being locked out of one of the most consequential supply chains shaping the future of global power.

item.title: Bigger Defense Budgets Are Not Enough to Secure Europe
item.description: Contrary to popular belief, the main obstacle to Europe’s strategic autonomy is not a lack of funding but its failure to develop critical technologies. Overcoming this constraint will require a fundamentally different approach to investment that better aligns public and private capital.

item.title: Swiss Karimova Corruption Case Discontinued
item.description: Karimova – already serving a 13-year sentence in Uzbekistan – wasn’t allowed to attend the trial in Switzerland and as such, the judges dismissed the case on procedural grounds.

item.title: India-US Relations Hit Another Bump
item.description: Trump’s reposting of a podcaster’s description of India as a “hellhole” is the latest of several insults from Washington.

item.title: Thailand Walked Into Its LNG Trap With Its Eyes Open – Long Before Hormuz
item.description: Bangkok’s gas bet was always going to hurt consumers. A distant conflict has merely made that reckoning impossible to defer.

item.title: The Bangladesh-US Trade Deal Is a Litmus Test for Dhaka’s Strategic Autonomy
item.description: The ART is framed as a market-access deal, but its deeper clauses are geopolitical. For Bangladesh, that represents a challenge to its traditional balancing strategy.

item.title: Preventing Online Child Abuse Should Be Easier
item.description: The international response to online child abuse continues to fall short. The problem is not that we lack known solutions or sufficient evidence to understand the challenge; it is that we have failed to apply existing tools fully, consistently, and in a globally coordinated fashion.

item.title: Former Kyrgyz Security Chief Tashiev Confirms Criminal Case Against Him
item.description: In his statement, Tashiev urges people not to protest and "act only within the law."

item.description: Josie Delap, our Middle East editor, reflects on the other facets of locations synonymous with strife

item.title: China’s Sanctions Hit Europe’s Emerging Drone Doctrine
item.description: Several European countries have been quietly developing a new drone doctrine built on both Ukraine’s battlefield experience and Taiwan’s high-tech components. China is trying to sever that link.

item.title: Can Central Asia Finally Coordinate on Climate and Water?
item.description: Central Asia is not yet a fully coordinated regional actor on climate and water, but it is starting to behave like one in some areas.

item.title: War Is Killing Ukraine’s Oligarchy
item.description: The fight against Russia is accomplishing what years of struggle against corruption couldn’t.

item.title: Hedging Is the New Normal
item.description: For the foreseeable future, successful statecraft will depend on hedging.

item.title: The EU Is the New Go-To Middle Power
item.description: In a world of disorder, the bloc’s boring stability is suddenly attractive.

item.title: Living Without an AI Kill Switch
item.description: With the arrival of Anthropic’s scarily powerful Mythos model, the tech titans that have long argued against any kind of AI regulation are now clamoring for it. Governments should take immediate steps to coordinate effectively and enable rapid information sharing, while also compelling developers to meet basic safety standards.

item.description: Washington has no good options for evacuating Americans in a crisis.

item.description: Zardari’s visit focused on CPEC’s second phase, exploring new avenues for bilateral trade and investment, and advancing regional connectivity.

item.description: The recent visit to China of Vietnam's top leader To Lam could well mark a turning point in the sometimes fraught bilateral relationship.

item.description: A whopping 2.7 million voters have been deleted from the electoral rolls in West Bengal after the controversial SIR exercise. Here are some of their stories.

item.title: The EU Needs New Security Partners
item.description: Europe’s security order needs to be reconstituted. In a world of fracturing alliances, closer security partnerships with a wider range of states, however small, will increasingly become a source of power, allowing Europe to defend its interests wherever they are at stake.

item.title: Asia’s Economic Diplomacy for Tumultuous Times
item.description: Instead of allowing external powers to dictate their development, China, India, Japan, South Korea, and Vietnam have balanced strategic international engagement with domestic capacity-building and decision-making. This model has become newly relevant for Canada, the United Kingdom, and other middle powers.

item.title: Expanding Frontiers: China’s Military Push Beyond the First Island Chain
item.description: Beijing has a deliberate, long-term strategy – one that prioritizes persistence and gradual advantage.

item.title: Whether Kusong or Yongbyon, North Korea Will Never Give Up Its Nuclear Weapons Program
item.description: A controversy over the South Korean unification minister’s comments exposes the illusion at the heart of decades of North Korea policy.

item.title: What’s Wrong With the SAVE America Act?
item.description: Much of the discussion about US President Donald Trump’s proposed SAVE America Act has centered on the bill’s partisan electoral implications. But given the inevitability of false positives, Americans should be asking a more fundamental question: Am I willing to give up my own right to vote to keep a non-citizen from voting?

item.title: Is the Indian Army Ready for the Next War?
item.description: Col. Ajai Shukla discusses India’s Army modernization and reform post-Sindoor.

item.title: High Oil Prices Are Devastating Island States – and Moving the Needle on Climate
item.description: For many small island states, the abstract concept of a “polycrisis” has become a dangerously tangible threat.

item.title: The Perils of South Korea’s Digital Diplomacy
item.description: Lee Jae-myung’s post on X marked a new beginning for South Korea’s digital diplomacy. It immediately caused a diplomatic row.

item.title: What Does Australia’s National Defense Strategy Mean for the Quad?
item.description: The implications of Australia’s NDS for the Indo-Pacific region and the Quad warrant careful examination.

item.title: South Korea’s Precarious Balancing Act
item.description: Caught between US President Donald Trump’s unpredictable administration and a rising China, South Korea is highly exposed to geopolitical turmoil and energy shocks. With American reliability in doubt, it must now safeguard its export-driven economy and build its own capabilities while navigating an increasingly unstable world.

item.title: China’s Belt and Road Initiative in Bangladesh, 10 Years Later
item.description: It’s been almost a decade since Bangladesh joined the BRI. What has the Belt and Road actually accomplished in the country?

item.title: Could Russia Mediate the Conflict Between Pakistan and Afghanistan?
item.description: Moscow is the latest to offer, however cautiously and conditionally, to mediate between Pakistan and Afghanistan.

item.title: Russia’s War Boom Masks an Economic Implosion
item.description: Record-low unemployment is the result of millions of missing workers.

item.title: Russia’s War Boom Masks an Economic Implosion
item.description: Record-low unemployment is the result of millions of missing workers.

item.title: Sun Tzu’s Advice for Donald Trump
item.description: Amid a series of massive, illegal policy blunders, US President Donald Trump has set his sights on stabilizing relations with Chinese President Xi Jinping at their May summit. But it will probably go poorly because Trump is incapable of understanding the importance of strategy, a lesson that China's leaders have long grasped.

item.title: Central Asia’s Next Growth Story Runs on Water It Does Not Have
